quiz_question: Which Nazi minister controlled all German media as propaganda minister?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Goebbels as Reich Minister of Propaganda controlled German newspapers, radio, film, and arts — he made radio the primary instrument of Nazi propaganda, with cheap 'People's Receivers' distributed widely.

quiz_question: In how many countries is Malay (or its standardized form) an official language?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Malay is an official language of Brunei, Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. In Indonesia, the standardized variety called Bahasa Indonesia is the official language and one of the working languages of Timor-Leste. Altogether, Malay is spoken by about 77 million people across Maritime Southeast Asia.

quiz_question: How many countries did Simón Bolívar liberate from Spanish colonial rule?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Bolívar's campaigns between 1810 and 1825 led to the independence of Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia (named after him), and Panama. He is the only person in history to have had a country named in his honor during his lifetime.

quiz_question: Which ancient Arab people built their kingdom centered in Petra?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the third century BC, Arab Nabataeans established their kingdom centered in Petra, the rose-red city carved directly into sandstone cliffs. Their trading empire thrived for centuries before Roman absorption.

quiz_question: In what year was Salvador's Pelourinho historic centre declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Salvador was founded in 1549 as the first capital of Colonial Brazil. Its Historic Centre (Pelourinho) was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1985 for its colonial architecture dating from the 17th century.

quiz_question: Why are ancient shipwrecks exceptionally preserved in the Black Sea's depths?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Black Sea has a permanent anoxic (oxygen-free) deep layer that does not drain or mix with surface water. This absence of oxygen prevents the biological decay that would normally destroy organic material, leaving ancient wooden ships perfectly preserved on the seafloor.

quiz_question: What is considered one of the world's most hunted game birds?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the common pheasant is one of the world's most hunted birds; it has been introduced for that purpose to many regions and is also common on game farms. It is perhaps the most widespread and ancient gamebird of more than regional importance in the whole world.

quiz_question: What happens when pet goldfish are released into the wild in places like Australia?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, goldfish released into the wild have become an invasive pest in parts of North America and Australia. Wild goldfish can grow much larger than their aquarium counterparts and disrupt native ecosystems.

quiz_question: What baked good is officially categorized as different from the American muffin?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the American quickbread muffin has an explicit 'different from: English muffin' property. Despite sharing a name, they are unrelated foods — one is a sweet cup-shaped cake, the other is a flat yeasted bread toasted on a griddle.

quiz_question: Approximately what percentage of world molybdenum production goes into steel alloys?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, molybdenum is a silvery metal with the sixth-highest melting point of any element. It readily forms hard, stable carbides in alloys, and for this reason most of the world production of the element — about 80% — is used in steel alloys, including high-strength alloys and superalloys.

quiz_question: What are the two major hormones produced by humans during stressful situations?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, when stressed, humans rely on two major systems: the SAM axis releases adrenaline via the sympathetic nervous system (fight-or-flight), while the HPA axis regulates cortisol, which influences metabolic, psychological, and immunological functions.

quiz_question: Which moon is the only one in the Solar System with a denser atmosphere than Earth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Titan is 'the only moon known to have a dense atmosphere — denser than Earth's — and is the only known object in the Solar System besides Earth with clear evidence of stable bodies of surface liquid.' These liquid bodies are seas of liquid methane and ethane, not water.

quiz_question: In which decade did Dürer establish his European reputation through woodcut prints?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Dürer established his reputation and influence across Europe in his twenties due to his high-quality woodcut prints, which were stylistically Gothic yet revolutionised the potential of that medium.

quiz_question: What early painting style did Velázquez use, known for dramatic dark and light contrasts?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Velázquez began to paint in a precise tenebrist style — dramatic use of dark and light — later developing a freer manner characterized by bold brushwork that influenced Impressionism centuries later.

quiz_question: Which ancient Sanskrit epic describes a citadel whose location may match Delhi's medieval fort Purana Qila?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the topography of the medieval fort Purana Qila on the banks of the Yamuna river in Delhi matches the literary description of the citadel Indraprastha in the Sanskrit epic Mahabharata. However, excavations in the area have revealed no signs of an ancient built environment to confirm this.

quiz_question: What percentage of the world's known nickel reserves does New Caledonia hold?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, New Caledonia contains approximately 25% of the world's nickel reserves. This makes the French special collectivity — a Pacific island group of just 270,000 people — one of the most economically significant mining territories on Earth.

quiz_question: Which severe complication can develop from encephalitis beyond its initial symptoms?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, encephalitis initial symptoms include headache, fever, confusion, and stiff neck. Complications can progress to seizures, hallucinations, trouble speaking, memory problems, and hearing difficulties. A weak immune system is a key risk factor.

quiz_question: Which French city is called the gastronomic capital of France?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Lyon has long been celebrated as France's food capital for its traditional bouchon restaurants and extraordinary density of acclaimed chefs.

quiz_question: What condition causes muscle weakness to return years after recovering from polio?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, years after recovery from polio, post-polio syndrome may occur — with a slow development of muscle weakness similar to what the person had during the initial infection. It's a late complication that can emerge decades after the original illness.

quiz_question: What geological process most commonly creates a waterfall?
distractors: Tidal surge cutting a cliff edge
explanation: Wikipedia explains the most common waterfall formation: 'a river courses over a top layer of resistant bedrock before falling onto softer rock, which erodes faster, leading to an increasingly high fall.' The differential erosion rates cause the hard layer to project outward over time.

quiz_question: What rank does Mongolia hold among the world's largest landlocked countries?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Mongolia covers 1,564,116 square kilometres, making it the 18th-largest country in the world overall and the second-largest landlocked country after Kazakhstan. Uniquely, it is also the largest landlocked country that does not border any inland sea.

quiz_question: Where is Ernest Rutherford buried, alongside Darwin and Newton?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, after Rutherford's death in 1937 he was buried in Westminster Abbey near Charles Darwin and Isaac Newton — an honor reserved for very few scientists. A New Zealand farm boy had joined the pantheon of science's greatest names.

quiz_question: What term describes any model or myth explaining the origin of the universe?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cosmogony (also spelled cosmogeny or cosmogenesis) is any model concerning the origin of the cosmos or the universe. It differs from cosmology, which studies the structure of the universe, and from eschatology, which concerns the universe's end. Wikidata notes it as a facet of the universe concept.

quiz_question: What event in 1479 created the nucleus of modern Spain by joining two crowns?
correct_answer: Death of John II of Aragon
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1479, upon John II of Aragon's death, the crowns of Aragon and Castile were united to form the nucleus of modern Spain. However, the Aragonese lands retained autonomous parliamentary and administrative institutions, such as the Corts, until Philip V's Nueva Planta decrees in 1707.

quiz_question: On what unusual calendar date was the dwarf planet Ceres first discovered?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Giuseppe Piazzi discovered Ceres on 1 January 1801 at the Palermo Astronomical Observatory in Sicily. He initially announced it as a new planet — but it was later reclassified as an asteroid, and then in 2006 as a dwarf planet.

quiz_question: Which German city is home to both Mercedes-Benz and Porsche?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Stuttgart is headquarters to Mercedes-Benz Group AG and Porsche AG, and hosts museums for both iconic automakers.

quiz_question: Why does an astronaut weigh less on the Moon but have the same mass?
correct_answer: Weight is a force; mass isn't
distractors: The Moon has less air pressure
explanation: According to Wikipedia, mass is an intrinsic property of a body while weight is a force — the product of mass and gravitational acceleration. Because the Moon's gravity is weaker, an object's weight is lower there, but its mass (resistance to acceleration) remains identical.

quiz_question: Approximately how many people live in Serbia, excluding Kosovo?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Serbia has approximately 6.6 million inhabitants not counting Kosovo, and Belgrade serves as the capital and largest city. Serbia formally adheres to the policy of military neutrality.

quiz_question: What happens to data stored in RAM when you turn off a computer?
distractors: It moves to the hard drive
explanation: According to Wikipedia, RAM is normally associated with volatile memory — stored information is lost if power is removed. This is why unsaved work in a program disappears if the computer crashes: it was held in RAM, not written to permanent storage.

quiz_question: Which composer's 1857–1859 opera was based on the 12th-century legend of Tristan and Isolde?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the name Isolde derives from either the Old High German words īs ('ice') and hiltja ('battle'), or the Brythonic adsiltia ('she who is gazed upon'). The name was popularized in German-speaking countries by Wagner's opera Tristan und Isolde, composed between 1857 and 1859 and based on the 12th-century chivalric romance Tristan and Iseult.

quiz_question: Until what year was the shallot classified as a separate species from the onion?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the shallot was reclassified in 2011 as a botanical variety of the common onion, Allium cepa. This is a striking example of how botanical taxonomy continues to be revised as science advances.

quiz_question: What is iodine deficiency the leading preventable cause of?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, iodine is required for the synthesis of thyroid hormones. Iodine deficiency affects about two billion people and is the leading preventable cause of intellectual disabilities. This is why table salt in many countries is iodized — it delivers the essential mineral to populations lacking seafood in their diet.

quiz_question: Which famous Muslim ruler did Maimonides serve as personal physician?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Maimonides advanced his vocations and became renowned as an astronomer, philosopher, and physician — even being appointed to serve as personal physician to Saladin of the Ayyubid Sultanate. This was a remarkable cross-religious professional relationship.

quiz_question: When was the field-effect transistor concept first proposed — 22 years before it could be built?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, physicist Julius Edgar Lilienfeld proposed the concept of a field-effect transistor (FET) in 1925, but it was not possible to construct a working device at that time. The first working transistor came in 1947.

quiz_question: How many islands does Italy have in addition to its mainland peninsula?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Italy consists of a peninsula that extends into the Mediterranean Sea, plus nearly 800 islands. The most notable are Sicily and Sardinia, which are among the largest islands in the Mediterranean.

quiz_question: What element is the standard window material in X-ray machines?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, beryllium's low density and atomic mass make it relatively transparent to X-rays and ionizing radiation, so it is the most common window material for X-ray equipment and particle detectors. Despite this peaceful use, inhaled beryllium dust can cause a life-threatening lung disease called berylliosis.

quiz_question: Which prefect did Napoleon III commission to reconstruct Paris with its famous wide boulevards?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Napoleon III commissioned a grand reconstruction of Paris carried out by prefect of the Seine, Georges-Eugène Haussmann. This urban renewal project created the wide boulevards, parks, and public buildings that define Paris's layout today.

quiz_question: Which country is credited as the birthplace of democracy and the Olympic Games?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Greece is considered the cradle of Western civilisation and the birthplace of democracy, Western philosophy, Western literature, historiography, political science, theatre, and the Olympic Games.

quiz_question: The Cook Islands' ocean exclusive zone is how many times larger than its land area?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the Cook Islands' land area is just 236.7 km², but their Exclusive Economic Zone covers 1,960,027 km² of ocean — about 8,280 times larger. This gives the tiny nation vast maritime resources and fishing rights.

quiz_question: Creation myths are the most common form of myth globally. In what tradition do they primarily develop, explaining why they typically have multiple versions?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, creation myths develop in oral traditions and therefore typically have multiple versions; found throughout human culture, they are the most common form of myth.

quiz_question: Which lifeform is credited with introducing the concept of symbiosis into biology?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, lichens are hybrid colonies of algae or cyanobacteria living symbiotically among filaments of multiple fungus species, also hosting bacteria. Lichens are the lifeform that first brought the term symbiosis (as Symbiotismus) into biological context. There are about 20,000 known lichen species. Some are among the oldest living things, and lichenometry — measuring their slow and regular growth rate — is used to date geological events.

quiz_question: What is Hawaii's distinction as a population center?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Hawaii sits in the central Pacific Ocean approximately 3,700 km from North America and 6,200 km from Asia. It is the most isolated archipelago in the world, formed entirely by volcanic activity.

quiz_question: Which Italian island is classified as one of the world's five Blue Zones for longevity?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Sardinia is part of the Blue Zone designation — regions with unusually high concentrations of centenarians. Sardinia's Nuoro province has one of the world's highest concentrations of male centenarians.

quiz_question: What vision advantage does the hammerhead shark's wide, flat head provide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the cephalofoil (T-shaped head) gives hammerhead sharks superior binocular vision and depth perception, plus increased surface area for electroreceptors. Multiple functions have been proposed for the cephalofoil including sensory reception, maneuvering, and prey manipulation.

quiz_question: What term describes the northern pike's distribution across the entire Northern Hemisphere?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, northern pike have a holarctic distribution — meaning they are found in fresh waters of the entire Northern Hemisphere, spanning North America, Europe, and Asia.

quiz_question: Where does the common quail spend its winters?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the common quail is mainly migratory, breeding in the western Palearctic (Europe and western Asia) and wintering in Africa and southern India. It is widespread in Europe and North Africa and is categorised by the IUCN as Least Concern.

quiz_question: What event marked the beginning of the Middle Ages?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the Middle Ages began in 476 CE with the fall of the Western Roman Empire. Wikipedia describes this as the start of the Early Middle Ages, after which new kingdoms formed from the remnants of Roman civilization.

quiz_question: How many Michelin stars has Gordon Ramsay's restaurant group been awarded in total?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Gordon Ramsay's restaurant group, Gordon Ramsay Restaurants (founded 1997), has been awarded 17 Michelin stars overall and currently holds eight. His flagship Restaurant Gordon Ramsay in Chelsea, London has held three Michelin stars — the maximum possible — since its opening.

quiz_question: What was IBM's original name when it was founded in 1911?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, IBM was founded in 1911 as the Computing-Tabulating-Recording Company (CTR), a holding company of manufacturers of record-keeping and measuring systems. It was renamed 'International Business Machines' in 1924 and soon became the leading manufacturer of punch-card tabulating systems.

quiz_question: Which Ottoman sultan had the longest reign in the empire's history?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Suleiman I ruled the Ottoman Empire from 1520 to 1566 — the longest reign among all Ottoman sultans. During his 46-year reign he raised the empire's subjects to at least 25 million people and presided over its political, military, and cultural peak.
